summary: I was raised in a traditional and well-known family in a village in Japan. Life for a wife there was very difficult, and I saw my mother suffer a lot. Since I was about 14 or 15 years old, I wanted to come to America because I didn't want to live in such a poverty-stricken Japan. When we visited Japan last time, we kept our visit quiet so as not to cause a big celebration, and my brothers, who worked at universities, were surprised to see us.

I came to the United States because I wanted a better life than what I had in Japan. My husband had just graduated from Wisconsin Davis in Agriculture, so we started our life here with little money but hope. I lived in Isleton, California, where our children attended segregated schools, and later they went to public schools around Sacramento. Despite initial difficulties, my children all succeeded in their education and careers in America.

My husband, Mr. Itano, had just graduated from the University of California, Davis in Agriculture when we came to America. He didn't have much money at that time, but my mother had said he would be able to feed me, and that turned out to be true. His education from UC Davis was an important part of our early life in the United States.

We lived in Isleton, California, where our children had to attend segregated schools. It was a challenging environment because the schools were separate for Japanese children, and many of the Nisei kids had heavy accents. People in Sacramento wondered if all young people in Isleton were Riper, but my son didn't know Japan at all. The segregated schooling affected their English skills initially.

Florin, California, was another place where there were segregated schools for Japanese children like in Isleton. I heard that Nisei students from Florin had heavy Japanese accents because of attending these segregated schools. This was part of the broader experience of Japanese Americans facing educational segregation in California at that time.

Around Sacramento, all Nisei children went to Lincoln Elementary School, which was not officially segregated but had many Japanese and Chinese children and very few Caucasians. The children were learning basic English phrases, and the teachers, like Miss Edna and the strict principal Miss Cook, worked hard to manage the students. The school environment reflected the challenges of Japanese American children adapting to English and American culture.

Sacramento was the city near where we lived and where many Japanese American families, including mine, sent their children to school. People in Sacramento were curious about the Japanese community, especially when we were relocated to Tule Lake. The city had a mix of Japanese and Chinese children in schools like Lincoln Elementary, and it was a place where the Japanese American experience was closely observed.

My son Masashi is a pathologist working at Long Beach Memorial Medical Center. It makes me proud that all my children have succeeded in their professions, with Masashi contributing to healthcare there. This shows how far our family has come since arriving in America with so little.

One of my acquaintances mentioned that their oldest daughter went to the University of California, Berkeley. It was a significant achievement for Japanese American families at the time, reflecting the emphasis we placed on education despite the hardships. Many Nisei children aspired to attend colleges like UC Berkeley to improve their futures.

My son George is a diplomat currently stationed in the Philippines. It's remarkable to see how our family has spread out and taken on important roles internationally. His work in the Philippines reflects the opportunities that have opened up for Japanese Americans over the years.

My second daughter was sent to Japan to attend Seishin Jogakuin, a women's college, before the war started. She came back just as World War II began, which disrupted her education. It was common for Japanese American families to send their children back to Japan for schooling, especially when work opportunities were limited here.

During the war, I was at the Tule Lake War Relocation Center, while my daughter Masako was in Minnesota. We had to decide whether to return to Japan or stay in America, and Masako chose to stay and serve America despite being alone. Tule Lake was a difficult place, but it was part of the forced relocation of Japanese Americans during World War II.

I knew a Nisei who graduated from Harvard University, but despite his education, the only job he could find was as a delivery boy. This showed the discrimination Japanese Americans faced even with top qualifications. It was a tough time, but many still valued education highly and encouraged their children to study hard.

World War II was a defining event for our community. Many Japanese Americans, including my family, were forcibly relocated to camps like Tule Lake. Despite the hardships, Nisei soldiers showed great patriotism and effort, inspired by the spirit taught by their parents. The war also exposed Japanese Americans to different parts of the country, helping others recognize their abilities.

I often read articles that quoted from the Bible, especially those by Motoko Hani in the magazine 'Fujisino Tomo.' She said she got ideas about education from the Bible, emphasizing that children should do their best and be kind, not just focus on winning. This was different from the usual Japanese parental advice of 'Don't get defeated,' and it influenced how some Nisei thought about effort and kindness.
